chunks.text: Witness Statement — Recorded under Section 161 CrPC Witness: Divya Shankar, Age 34, Relationship Manager, Indian Overseas Bank, T. Nagar Branch Recorded on: 20 March 2024 | Investigating Officer: Sub-Inspector R. Vignesh Statement I am employed as Relationship Manager at Indian Overseas Bank, T. Nagar Branch, since 2018. I handled the opening of the joint current account bearing number ending 4471 in the names of Mr. Rajesh Kumar Iyer and Mr. Suresh Babu Menon. As per our bank records, the account was opened on 3 January 2024. The mandate on file specifies 'Either or Survivor' operation, meaning either signatory could operate the account independently, including withdrawals of any amount, without requiring the co-signature of the other party. I confirm that a withdrawal of Rs. 3,40,000 was made on 22 January 2024 via a cheque signed solely by Mr. Suresh Babu Menon. I state clearly that no joint signature was required or obtained for this transaction, as the account mandate did not mandate dual authorisation at any amount. I further confirm from our records that no deposit of Rs. 12,50,000, or any amount close to it, was ever credited to this account by Mr. Menon between January and March 2024. The only credit entries in this period were the initial Rs. 50,000 deposit on 3 January 2024 and a subsequent Rs. 15,000 deposit on 10 February 2024, source unspecified in our records.

Evidence & Exhibit Log Case No. 214/2024 | Compiled by: Investigating Officer, T. Nagar Police Station Exhibit A — Memorandum of Understanding (3 January 2024) Clause 4 of the MoU states: 'Party B (Suresh Babu Menon) shall deposit Rs. 12,50,000 into the joint operating account within 15 days of execution of this agreement.' Clause 7 states the account shall operate on an 'Either or Survivor' basis for transactions up to Rs. 2,00,000 only, and shall require joint signature for any withdrawal exceeding Rs. 2,00,000 — this appears to conflict with the bank's own mandate record described in Exhibit C. Exhibit B — Bank Statement (1 Jan 2024 to 10 Mar 2024) Opening balance: Rs. 0. Credit of Rs. 50,000 on 3 Jan 2024. Credit of Rs. 15,000 on 10 Feb 2024. Debit of Rs. 3,40,000 on 22 Jan 2024, cheque no. 002841, single signatory: S. B. Menon. Closing balance as of 10 Mar 2024: Rs. -2,75,000 (overdraft). Exhibit C — Bank Account Mandate Form Operating instructions field marked: 'Either or Survivor — No Limit'. Signed by both parties on 3 January 2024. This directly contradicts Clause 7 of the MoU (Exhibit A), which purports to cap single-signatory withdrawals at Rs. 2,00,000. Exhibit D — WhatsApp Transcript (20 Jan - 1 Mar 2024) 20 Jan 2024, 11:02 - Rajesh Iyer: 'Suresh, the deposit was due two days ago, please confirm status.' 20 Jan 2024, 18:45 - Suresh Menon: 'Processing it this week, minor delay on my end.' 5 Feb 2024, 09:15 - Rajesh Iyer: 'Still no deposit and I see a large withdrawal instead. Explain.' 5 Feb 2024, 20:03 - Suresh Menon: 'I needed it for an emergency, will return by month end.' 1 Mar 2024, 16:30 - Rajesh Iyer: 'You have not returned anything. I am going to the police.' 1 Mar 2024, 16:52 - Suresh Menon: 'Do what you want, the account was mine to operate.' Note on Discrepancies Investigating officer flags two items for legal review: (i) the conflicting withdrawal-limit clauses between the MoU and the bank mandate form; and (ii) a discrepancy in witness recollection regarding the date the complainant first raised concerns internally (25 January per Anand Prakash's statement) versus the complainant's own FIR narrative, which does not mention this internal date at all and instead centres on the 1 March 2024 confrontation.

form; and (ii) a discrepancy in witness recollection regarding the date the complainant first raised concerns internally (25 January per Anand Prakash's statement) versus the complainant's own FIR narrative, which does not mention this internal date at all and instead centres on the 1 March 2024 confrontation.

First Information Report — Case No. 214/2024 Police Station: T. Nagar | District: Chennai | State: Tamil Nadu Date of FIR: 14 March 2024 | Time: 10:40 hrs 1. Complainant Details Name: Rajesh Kumar Iyer, S/o Krishnan Iyer, Age 42, Occupation: Proprietor, M/s Iyer Textiles Pvt Ltd, No. 18 Usman Road, T. Nagar, Chennai - 600017. 2. Accused Name: Suresh Babu Menon, S/o Gopalakrishna Menon, Age 37, Occupation: Business Partner, residing at Flat 4B, Kamala Towers, Anna Nagar, Chennai - 600040. 3. Sections of Law Offence registered under Section 420 (Cheating), Section 406 (Criminal Breach of Trust), and Section 120B (Criminal Conspiracy) of the Indian Penal Code, 1860. 4. Brief Facts of the Case The complainant states that on 3 January 2024, he entered into a partnership agreement with the accused for the supply of textile machinery, under which the accused was to deposit a sum of Rs. 12,50,000 (Rupees Twelve Lakh Fifty Thousand only) as working capital into the joint business account maintained at Indian Overseas Bank, T. Nagar Branch, Account No. XXXXXX4471. The complainant further states that as per the signed Memorandum of Understanding dated 3 January 2024, the accused was required to deposit the amount within 15 days, i.e. on or before 18 January 2024. The complainant alleges that despite repeated reminders on 20 January, 5 February, and 28 February 2024, the accused failed to deposit the said amount and instead withdrew Rs. 3,40,000 from the joint account on 22 January 2024 without the complainant's consent. The complainant states he personally visited the accused's residence on 1 March 2024 to discuss the matter, at which time the accused allegedly admitted verbally to having used the funds for personal purposes and promised repayment within a week. No repayment was made. 5. Witnesses Cited (i) Mr. Anand Prakash, Accountant, Iyer Textiles Pvt Ltd (ii) Ms. Divya Shankar, Bank Relationship Manager, Indian Overseas Bank, T. Nagar Branch 6. Documents Enclosed 1. Memorandum of Understanding dated 3 January 2024 (Annexure A) 2. Bank statement of joint account for period 1 Jan 2024 to 10 Mar 2024 (Annexure B)
3. WhatsApp chat transcript between complainant and accused, 20 Jan - 1 Mar 2024 (Annexure C)

Witness Statement — Recorded under Section 161 CrPC Witness: Anand Prakash, Age 29, Accountant, Iyer Textiles Pvt Ltd Recorded on: 18 March 2024 | Investigating Officer: Sub-Inspector R. Vignesh Statement I have been employed as accountant with Iyer Textiles Pvt Ltd since June 2021. I handle the day-to-day bookkeeping and bank reconciliation for the firm, including the joint account opened with Mr. Suresh Babu Menon at Indian Overseas Bank, T. Nagar Branch. I state that the joint account was opened on 3 January 2024 with an initial deposit of Rs. 50,000 made by the complainant, Mr. Rajesh Kumar Iyer. I confirm that as per the account statements I reviewed, Mr. Suresh Babu Menon did not deposit the agreed sum of Rs. 12,50,000 at any point between January and March 2024. I further state that on 22 January 2024, a withdrawal of Rs. 3,40,000 was made from the joint account. To the best of my knowledge, this withdrawal was authorised by both signatories, since the account required joint signatures for any withdrawal above Rs. 1,00,000. I did not personally witness who initiated the withdrawal request at the bank counter. I recall that Mr. Iyer informed me around 25 January 2024, not in March as he may have stated elsewhere, that he suspected something was wrong with the account, and asked me to prepare a detailed reconciliation, which I completed by 2 February 2024. I am aware of at least two reminder emails sent to Mr. Menon, dated 20 January and 5 February 2024. I am not aware of any reminder sent on 28 February 2024 as I did not draft or send one on that date.

analysis.summary:
## Executive Summary
The case involves a dispute between Rajesh Kumar Iyer and Suresh Babu Menon, who entered into a partnership agreement for the supply of textile machinery. Menon was supposed to deposit Rs. 12,50,000 into their joint business account but failed to do so and instead withdrew Rs. 3,40,000 without Iyer's consent. The key legal dispute revolves around the operation of the joint account, the mandate for withdrawals, and the alleged breach of trust by Menon. The investigation involves witness statements from Divya Shankar, the bank's relationship manager, and Anand Prakash, the accountant for Iyer Textiles Pvt Ltd.

## Key Statutory Sections & Case Law
The case involves sections 420 (Cheating), 406 (Criminal Breach of Trust), and 120B (Criminal Conspiracy) of the Indian Penal Code, 1860. These sections are relevant as they pertain to the alleged cheating and breach of trust by Menon. Additionally, the Indian Contract Act, 1872, may be applicable in interpreting the partnership agreement and the Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) between the parties. The Supreme Court's judgments on the interpretation of contractual agreements and the principles of partnership law may also be relevant in this case.

## Legal Web Leads
The legal web search did not yield any usable results for this query. However, a thorough analysis of the provided PDF excerpts and the application of relevant Indian statutes and case law can still provide valuable insights into the case.

## Contradictions & Inconsistencies
1. The MoU (Exhibit A) states that the account shall operate on an 'Either or Survivor' basis for transactions up to Rs. 2,00,000 only, and shall require joint signature for any withdrawal exceeding Rs. 2,00,000 (Excerpt 4, Page 1). However, the bank account mandate form (Exhibit C) signed by both parties on 3 January 2024, specifies 'Either or Survivor — No Limit' (Excerpt 4, Page 1).
2. Divya Shankar's witness statement (Excerpt 1, Page 1) confirms that the account was opened with an 'Either or Survivor' operation, meaning either signatory could operate the account independently, including withdrawals of any amount, without requiring the co-signature of the other party. This contradicts the MoU's clause 7, which requires joint signatures for withdrawals above Rs. 2,00,000.
3. Anand Prakash's witness statement (Excerpt 2, Page 1) states that the withdrawal of Rs. 3,40,000 on 22 January 2024 was authorised by both signatories, as the account required joint signatures for any withdrawal above Rs. 1,00,000. However, Divya Shankar's statement and the bank records (Excerpt 1, Page 1) confirm that the withdrawal was made solely by Suresh Babu Menon.
4. The complainant's FIR narrative (Excerpt 3, Page 1) does not mention the internal date (25 January 2024) when he first raised concerns about the account, as stated by Anand Prakash (Excerpt 2, Page 1).
5. The WhatsApp chat transcript (Excerpt 4, Page 1, and Excerpt 7, Page 2) shows that Menon admitted to using the funds for an emergency and promised to return them by the end of the month. However, there is no record of Menon making any such repayment.

## Missing Facts & Evidence Gaps
The case lacks concrete evidence of the partnership agreement's terms and conditions beyond the MoU. Additionally, there is no direct testimony from Menon regarding his actions and intentions. The investigation could benefit from obtaining more detailed financial records, including any transactions related to the alleged emergency that Menon claimed to have used the funds for. Furthermore, witness statements from other individuals who may have been involved in the partnership or had knowledge of the transactions could provide valuable insights.

## Strategic Insights & Next Steps
To strengthen the case against Menon, it is essential to obtain more concrete evidence of his intentions and actions. This could involve subpoenaing Menon's personal financial records to trace the funds withdrawn from the joint account. Additionally, interviewing other witnesses who may have been involved in the partnership or had knowledge of the transactions could provide valuable insights. The prosecution should also focus on establishing the contradiction between the MoU and the bank account mandate form, as this discrepancy could be crucial in determining the legitimacy of Menon's actions. Furthermore, the WhatsApp chat transcript should be thoroughly analyzed to demonstrate Menon's admission of guilt and his failure to repay the funds as promised. [UNCERTAIN] The exact strategy may depend on the further investigation and the collection of more evidence.
